title: A Casserole for the Missing
tagline: Love lives here. So does arson.
omen: a lake bell ringing without wind

beat_sheet.text: Wyatt Vale relocates to Alder Ridge after an emotionally negligent implosion and pretends work explains everything while quietly inheriting meditation annex nobody uses in winter. Wine is present like a municipal policy failure. Emotional exposition arrives on schedule with insanity multiplier 1.00.

Daniel Mercer hates the move and immediately distrusts Cole Sutton, a hardware store owner with suspiciously healthy handyman energy and access to church basement described as retreat supplies. Rachel Pike hesitates before explaining old family office described as seasonal materials. Emotional exposition arrives on schedule with insanity multiplier 1.07. The sheriff sees fresh mud on the boat key.

A wine scene at The Candle Finch introduces the snow globe, local gossip about the Christmas pageant incident, and the fact that everybody here has somewhere to hide from a conversation. Wine is present like a municipal policy failure. Wyatt Vale hesitates before explaining meditation annex nobody uses in winter. Emotional exposition arrives on schedule with insanity multiplier 1.14.

Jenna Mercer reveals a perfectly phrased warning in a well-lit kitchen while a lake bell ringing without wind, then declines to explain marina tackle room described as paperwork I have not gone through yet. Someone mentions the basement as if it were a legal relative. Jenna Mercer hesitates before explaining marina tackle room described as paperwork I have not gone through yet. Emotional exposition arrives on schedule with insanity multiplier 1.23. The sheriff sees fresh mud on the boat key.

A cliffside emotional exposition reframes overbearing mother tension as a civic hazard and makes old family office described as seasonal materials feel spiritually relevant. Wine is present like a municipal policy failure. Emotional exposition arrives on schedule with insanity multiplier 1.31.

Wyatt Vale snoops through a laptop, finds nothing conclusive, and becomes more certain anyway after she sees a dusty key labeled in handwriting that feels rehearsed inside meditation annex nobody uses in winter. Wine is present like a municipal policy failure. Cole Winterson hesitates before explaining marina tackle room described as paperwork I have not gone through yet. Emotional exposition arrives on schedule with insanity multiplier 1.40.

The town offers a fake sense of safety through a fundraiser, a casserole, and a sheriff who suspects the wrong person first while ignoring old family office described as seasonal materials. Someone mentions the basement as if it were a legal relative. Rachel Pike hesitates before explaining old family office described as seasonal materials. Emotional exposition arrives on schedule with insanity multiplier 1.50. The sheriff sees fresh mud on the boat key.

Cole Sutton is accused, gaslighted, then gently framed near hydrangeas until the boat key turns up somewhere impossible inside church basement described as retreat supplies. Wine is present like a municipal policy failure. Emotional exposition arrives on schedule with insanity multiplier 1.61.

A rain argument escalates into distributed secrecy infrastructure and the fake moral theme: 'A town never forgets. It just redecorates.' Rachel Pike hesitates before explaining old family office described as seasonal materials. Emotional exposition arrives on schedule with insanity multiplier 1.72. Someone who should be dead appears at brunch.

Someone goes missing after mentioning the unopened letter, the old orchard chapel, and marina tackle room described as paperwork I have not gone through yet in the same sentence. Wine is present like a municipal policy failure. Someone mentions the basement as if it were a legal relative. Jenna Mercer hesitates before explaining marina tackle room described as paperwork I have not gone through yet. Emotional exposition arrives on schedule with insanity multiplier 1.84.

The reveal ties unopened letter to Blackthorn Vineyard, St. Agnes Regional, and a brunch resurrection event by routing through old family office described as seasonal materials instead of one simple basement. Wine is present like a municipal policy failure. Emotional exposition arrives on schedule with insanity multiplier 1.97.

Daniel Mercer spots the timeline error that every adult has spiritually rented around and asks why everybody in this county owns just storage. Someone mentions the basement as if it were a legal relative. Cole Sutton hesitates before explaining church basement described as retreat supplies. Emotional exposition arrives on schedule with insanity multiplier 2.10. A phone vibrates from inside the wall.

A frantic drive toward Bellweather Sheriff's Office ends with ambulance lights, a one-line empowerment speech, and a locked door inside marina tackle room described as paperwork I have not gone through yet. Wine is present like a municipal policy failure. Emotional exposition arrives on schedule with insanity multiplier 2.25.

Jenna Mercer confesses just enough to make the truth worse while the sheriff arrives exactly fourteen minutes too late and someone stares through the dusty window of church basement described as retreat supplies. Sienna Vale hesitates before explaining old family office described as donation overflow. Emotional exposition arrives on schedule with insanity multiplier 2.41. The ceramic owl is back on the kitchen island.

Sequel bait lands when the ceramic owl reappears and someone whispers about the Winterson case, the vineyard fire, and another offsite room nobody has adequately explained. Wine is present like a municipal policy failure. Someone mentions the basement as if it were a legal relative. Emotional exposition arrives on schedule with insanity multiplier 2.58. A basement light switches on in an empty house.

contradiction_ledger.entry: Cole Sutton is called Daniel in one kitchen scene and Derek during the rain argument.
Wyatt Vale says Bellweather is in Vermont, then calls it outside Atlanta after two glasses of wine.
A dead best friend appears at brunch and nobody agrees whether this is denial, evil, or scheduling.
The basement belongs to a condo, a lake house, and an abandoned vineyard at different times in Alder Ridge.

dialogue_samples: "You think because you wear flannel and own a dock light I am supposed to trust you, Cole?"
"Mothers do not disappear, Daniel. They evaporate into paperwork."
"I checked his social media likes, Wyatt. Nobody who enjoys motivational quotes is innocent."
"The snow globe is not decorative anymore and you know it."
"Trust is just fear with a casserole dish, Jenna."
"You seem upset," he said, making tea in a way that felt prosecutable.
"If that basement is innocent, why does it sound defensive?"
"I did not lie. I just told the truth in the order this town could survive."
fake_critic_blurb: "A Casserole for the Missing understands that menace is a regional utility." - County Suspense Quarterly
sequel_hook: Channel 8 Night Report hints that the vineyard fire had a witness who still hosts brunch.
synopsis: In the wind‑laden, rain‑soaked town of Blackthorn County, Wyatt Vale moves into a winter‑dead meditation annex and stumbles upon a dusty key that could unlock a family’s buried sins. Her overextended career as a magazine editor clashes with the quiet desperation of an overbearing mother who left her only in whispers and snow globes. Cole Sutton, the hardware store owner with a soft spot for church basements, becomes both romantic interest and potential suspect as he quietly knows where evidence should be hidden. The town’s lake bell rings without wind, echoing secrets in the hollow of the abandoned orchard chapel, while Jenna Mercer—widow, elegant wildcard—holds the key to a brunch that resurrects a dead best friend. As wine is poured at every gathering, the community’s “municipal policy failures” become ritualistic confessions, and the basement morphs into a legal relative, a condo, and an abandoned vineyard—all in one breath. Trust is rented, not given; it’s a lease on fear that threatens to evaporate as quickly as the mist over the docks.
scene_paragraphs:
Scene 1: Wyatt Vale sits at her new desk in the winter‑abandoned annex, the snow globe spinning like an omen. She sips wine while listening to the lake bell ring without wind, a sound that feels less like nature and more like a warning. The annex’s silence is broken by a faint click—her laptop chimes with no email. The scene sets a tone of unease, layering her professional façade over personal grief.
Scene 2: Daniel Mercer storms in, his distrust of Cole Sutton palpable. He slams the door, and the sheriff peeks from behind a stack of paperwork, spotting fresh mud on the boat key. A tense exchange about hardware stores and church basements follows, underscoring the town’s blurred lines between sanctuary and crime scene.
Scene 3: The Candle Finch hosts a wine‑filled party where Wyatt accidentally drops the snow globe. Conversations about the Christmas pageant incident circulate like wildfire. Jenna Mercer glides in with a perfectly phrased warning—her kitchen lights flicker as if echoing the lake bell’s silence.
Scene 4: A cliffside confrontation reveals overbearing mother tension; Rachel Pike speaks of the vineyard fire, hinting at deeper scars. The basement is described as a legal relative—a metaphor for hidden truths that people keep under their roofs.
Scene 5: In Act 2, Wyatt discovers a dusty key in her annex, written by hand, pointing to meditation rooms and secrets. Cole Winterson hesitates to explain the marina tackle room, creating a cliffhanger as wine pours again like municipal policy failures.
Scene 6: The finale’s frantic drive ends with ambulance lights flashing outside Bellweather Sheriff’s Office. Jenna confesses enough to deepen the mystery while Sienna Vale delivers an old family office donation overflow revelation, leaving viewers yearning for more.
